title: Why Transcripts Beat Show Notes
hook: Tomas Ek built a search engine over his own listening history — and found the real blocker was transcripts, not search.
takeaways: Only ~1 in 50 podcast feeds publishes a real transcript; show notes are marketing copy and you can't search aspirations.
His transcript cascade: published RSS transcript first, YouTube auto-captions second, local Whisper overnight as the last resort (~a third of episodes).
The economics flipped around 2024 when consumer laptops began transcribing faster than real time — the hard part became plumbing, not compute.
Once episodes become greppable pages, listening stops being ephemeral; he annotates claims and tracks which guests turned out to be right.
The transcript isn't the product — the accumulated cross-references are.

quotes.text: Show notes tell you what the producer hoped the episode was about; the transcript tells you what was actually said.
A transcript on its own is a haystack. Two hundred transcripts with a wiki on top is a memory.

people.name: Tomas Ek
people.note: Built Earhorn, a personal search engine over his own podcast listening history

topics.name: Podcast Transcripts
topics.why: Transcripts versus show notes; the rss → YouTube captions → Whisper cascade.

topics.name: Personal Knowledge Bases
topics.why: Two hundred transcripts with a wiki on top is a memory — listening that compounds instead of evaporating.

topics.name: Full-Text Search
topics.why: Finding the sentence, not the summary: search over everything you've ever heard.
